113‚Üí---
114‚Üí
115‚Üí## Backend Layers
116‚Üí
117‚Üí### Lucky5.Domain ‚Äî Core Engine (`Game/CleanRoom/`)
118‚Üí
119‚ÜíThis is the heart of the system. All files here are pure logic with no external dependencies.
120‚Üí
121‚Üí| File | Purpose |
122‚Üí|------|---------|
123‚Üí| `CoreModels.cs` | Enums and records: `HandCategory`, `PaytableProfile`, `RoundState`, card representations |
124‚Üí| `DeterministicRng.cs` | SplitMix64 seeded RNG ‚Äî all randomness flows through this |
125‚Üí| `FiveCardDrawEngine.cs` | Deal 5 cards, evaluate holds, draw replacements, evaluate final hand |
126‚Üí| `Lucky5DoubleUpEngine.cs` | Hi-Lo double-up loop: dealer card, guess, switch, 5‚ôÝ switch-only trigger, SafeFail, MachineClosed |
127‚Üí| `MachinePolicy.cs` | RTP management: distribution modes (COLD/NEUTRAL/HOT), deck alteration, payout scaling |
128‚Üí| `LineageProfiles.cs` | Cabinet variant profiles (Golden Poker, Bonus Poker lineage) |
129‚Üí| `PresentationNoiseGenerator.cs` | Separate noise seed for timing/visual variance |
130‚Üí

132‚Üí
133‚Üí| Entity | Purpose |
134‚Üí|--------|---------|
135‚Üí| `GameRound` | Single hand lifecycle: deal ‚Üí draw ‚Üí optional double-up ‚Üí settle. Tracks `WinAmount`, `OriginalWinAmount`, `IsPayoutSettled` |
136‚Üí| `MachineLedgerState` | Per-machine state: wallet balance, RTP tracking (CapitalIn/Out), jackpot values, streak counters, distribution mode |
137‚Üí| `User` | Player identity and authentication |
138‚Üí| `Machine` | Machine configuration |
139‚Üí| `PokerCard` | Card representation (suit + rank) |
140‚Üí
141‚Üí### Lucky5.Application ‚Äî Contracts & DTOs
142‚Üí
143‚ÜíService interfaces (`IGameService`, `IAuthService`) and all API data transfer objects. The DTOs define the exact shape of every API response.
144‚Üí
145‚ÜíKey DTOs:
146‚Üí- `DealResultDto` ‚Äî Cards dealt, round ID, jackpot state, advised holds
147‚Üí- `DrawResultDto` ‚Äî Final hand, win amount, hand rank, updated jackpots
148‚Üí- `DoubleUpResultDto` ‚Äî Guess result, current amount, wallet balance, status
149‚Üí
150‚Üí### Lucky5.Infrastructure ‚Äî Service Implementations

211‚Üí## Game Rules & Mechanics
212‚Üí
213‚Üí### Basic Flow
214‚Üí
215‚Üí1. **Bet** ‚Äî Player places bet (5,000‚Äì10,000, increment 100)
216‚Üí2. **Deal** ‚Äî 5 cards dealt; the machine session must already hold enough credits for both deal and draw
217‚Üí3. **Hold/Draw** ‚Äî Player selects holds (auto-hold suggests optimal); draw costs another bet
218‚Üí4. **Evaluate** ‚Äî Hand ranked, payout calculated per Lebanese paytable
219‚Üí5. **Win/Lose** ‚Äî If win, player can Take Score or enter Double-Up
220‚Üí6. **Double-Up** ‚Äî Optional Hi-Lo game to multiply winnings
221‚Üí
222‚Üí### Paytable (Lebanese Profile)
223‚Üí
224‚Üí| Hand | Multiplier |
225‚Üí|------|-----------|
226‚Üí| Royal Flush | 1000x |
227‚Üí| Straight Flush | 75x |
228‚Üí| Four of a Kind | 15x |
229‚Üí| Full House | 12x |
230‚Üí| Flush | 10x |
231‚Üí| Straight | 8x |
232‚Üí| Three of a Kind | 3x |
233‚Üí| Two Pair | 2x |
234‚Üí
235‚ÜíMinimum qualifying hand is **Two Pair**. Single pair does not pay.
236‚Üí
237‚Üí### Betting
238‚Üí
239‚Üí- Min: 5,000 / Max: 10,000 / Step: 100
240‚Üí- **Double deduction**: Deal costs 1x bet, Draw costs another 1x bet (total 2x per round)
241‚Üí- After completing a hand, first BET press resets to 5,000
242‚Üí- Starting credits: 200,000 (demo mode)
243‚Üí
244‚Üí### Auto-Hold
245‚Üí
246‚ÜíAfter dealing, the engine suggests optimal holds:
247‚Üí- Priority: quads > full house > flush > straight > trips > two pair > 4-to-flush > 4-to-straight > pair
248‚Üí- Player can override by clicking individual cards

251‚Üí
252‚Üí## Credit Accounting (Deferred Settlement)
253‚Üí
254‚ÜíThis is a critical architectural decision. Draw wins are **not** immediately credited to the player's wallet.
255‚Üí
256‚Üí### How It Works
257‚Üí
258‚Üí1. `DrawAsync` evaluates the hand and records `WinAmount` on the `GameRound` ‚Äî but does **not** add it to the wallet balance
259‚Üí2. The WIN indicator displays the pending win separately from CREDITS
260‚Üí3. Settlement (wallet credit) only occurs at:
261‚Üí   - **Take Score** ‚Äî Full cashout, triggers drain animation
262‚Üí   - **Take Half** ‚Äî Instant partial cashout (half to wallet, half continues in double-up)
263‚Üí   - **FinalizeDoubleUp** ‚Äî Double-up ends (win or lose)
264‚Üí
265‚Üí### Key Fields on GameRound
266‚Üí
267‚Üí| Field | Purpose |
268‚Üí|-------|---------|
269‚Üí| `WinAmount` | Current win value (may change via Take Half) |
270‚Üí| `OriginalWinAmount` | Immutable original draw win (for ledger delta tracking) |
271‚Üí| `IsPayoutSettled` | Idempotency guard ‚Äî prevents double-crediting on repeated cashout calls |
272‚Üí
273‚Üí### Why Deferred Settlement?
274‚Üí
275‚Üí- Base paytable wins stay gamble-eligible
276‚Üí- Jackpot wins go straight to machine credits and never enter double-up
277‚Üí- `SettledAmount` prevents partial-cashout accounting bugs
278‚Üí- `IsPayoutSettled` prevents double-cashout exploits
279‚Üí

282‚Üí## Progressive Jackpots
283‚Üí
284‚ÜíFour jackpot slots are tracked per machine:
285‚Üí
286‚Üí| Slot | Trigger Condition | Max Value | Reset Value (20%) |
287‚Üí|------|------------------|-----------|-------------------|
288‚Üí| Full House (FH) | FH trips rank matches player's chosen rank | 25,000,000 | 5,000,000 |
289‚Üí| Four of a Kind A (4K-A) | 4K hit while slot A is active | 999,999 | 200,000 |

297‚Üí- Machines can start at max jackpots

320‚Üí## Double-Up (Hi-Lo)
321‚Üí
322‚ÜíAfter any winning hand, the player can enter an inline Hi-Lo game to multiply their winnings.
323‚Üí
324‚Üí### Mechanics
325‚Üí
326‚Üí1. A **dealer card** is shown (face up)
327‚Üí2. Player guesses **BIG** (8‚ÄìK) or **SMALL** (2‚Äì6)
328‚Üí3. A **player card** is drawn:
329‚Üí   - Correct guess: win amount doubles
330‚Üí   - Wrong guess: lose everything
331‚Üí   - **Ace**: always wins regardless of guess direction
332‚Üí   - **7**: always loses (push/lose)
333‚Üí
334‚Üí### Switch Mechanic
335‚Üí
336‚Üí- Player can **switch** the dealer card up to **2 times** per round
337‚Üí- Each switch consumes the next card from the deterministic deck
338‚Üí- Strategic: allows avoiding unfavorable dealer cards
339‚Üí
340‚Üí### Five of Spades (5‚ôÝ) ‚Äî Lucky Card
341‚Üí
342‚ÜíWhen the **5 of Spades** appears as the dealer card (after a switch):
343‚Üí- Win amount is multiplied by **4x**
344‚Üí- **No-Lose mode** activates for that round
345‚Üí- If the player guesses wrong: **SafeFail** ‚Äî the current amount is banked instead of lost
346‚Üí- Visual: yellow flash, "LUCKY 5" banner
347‚Üí
348‚Üí### MachineClosed
349‚Üí
350‚ÜíIf machine credits reach or pass **50,000,000** through the final double-up jump:
351‚Üí- the current double-up result cashes out automatically into machine credits
352‚Üí- the machine is marked as closed for that player session
353‚Üí- the machine ledger itself does not reset
354‚Üí- player can cash out machine credits back to the lobby wallet subject to the machine-session rules
355‚Üí
356‚Üí### Take Score vs Take Half
357‚Üí
358‚Üí| Action | Effect |
359‚Üí|--------|--------|
360‚Üí| Take Score | Ends double-up, full amount drains to credits with animation |
361‚Üí| Take Half | Instantly takes half to credits (no animation), continues double-up with remaining half |
362‚Üí
363‚ÜíTake Half does **not** stop the double-up. If the remaining half is 0, it exits to idle.

367‚Üí## RTP & Machine Policy
368‚Üí
369‚ÜíTarget RTP: **87.5%** across all machines.
370‚Üí
371‚Üí### How RTP Is Achieved
372‚Üí
373‚ÜíThe natural base RTP from the paytable + double deduction is ~38%. The Machine Policy layer bridges this to 87.5% through two mechanisms:
374‚Üí
375‚Üí1. **Deck Composition Alteration** ‚Äî Before dealing, the policy can add or remove cards:
376‚Üí   - **COLD mode**: Removes high cards and 5‚ôÝ, making wins less likely
377‚Üí   - **NEUTRAL mode**: Standard deck
378‚Üí   - **HOT mode**: Injects extra 5‚ôÝ and high cards, making wins more likely
379‚Üí
380‚Üí2. **Payout Scaling** ‚Äî Win payouts are multiplied by a scale factor (~2.3x base):
381‚Üí   - Small wins (2P, 3K): ~0.96x of the current base scale
382‚Üí   - Medium wins (ST, FL, FH): ~1.02x of the current base scale
383‚Üí   - Big wins (4K, SF, RF): ~1.08x of the current base scale
384‚Üí
385‚ÜíThe current live policy also subtracts observed **jackpot RTP** from the base-game scaling target. That prevents the old failure mode where jackpots pushed the real RTP far above target while the scale logic still behaved as if only paytable wins existed.
386‚Üí
387‚Üí### Streak Management
388‚Üí
389‚ÜíThe policy tracks player win/loss streaks to create natural-feeling rhythm:
390‚Üí- `ConsecutiveLosses` counter biases toward HOT mode after 5+ losses
391‚Üí- After 15+ consecutive losses: forced HOT mode with maximum deck alteration
392‚Üí- After a win: 2‚Äì4 round cooldown before allowing next HOT phase
393‚Üí- Tracks `RoundsSinceMediumWin` for medium-tier pacing
394‚Üí
395‚Üí### Final QA note
396‚Üí
397‚Üí- jackpot wins now feed directly into machine credits and never enter double-up
398‚Üí- machine close is only evaluated on a real collecting event (cashout / final winning jump), not merely because a switched Lucky 5 preview amount looks large on screen
399‚Üí- extra Lucky 5 pressure in double-up now leans more on **5‚ôÝ** than on free ace-heavy decks, which keeps the feature visible without giving away as many automatic wins
400‚Üí

532‚Üí### Key Design Decisions
533‚Üí
534‚Üí1. **Clean-room approach**: Game logic was independently reimplemented based on behavioral analysis, not code copying
535‚Üí2. **Deterministic engine**: All randomness flows through SplitMix64 with known seeds ‚Äî every outcome is replayable
536‚Üí3. **Backend-authoritative**: Frontend is a thin display layer; all game state lives on the server
537‚Üí4. **Deferred settlement**: Wins are not credited until explicitly cashed out (prevents accounting bugs)
538‚Üí5. **Lebanese paytable**: Uses the specific multiplier profile from the original machines (Two Pair minimum, DEAL=red/BET=green convention)
539‚Üí
540‚Üí
541‚Üí## Machine Credit vs Wallet
542‚Üí
543‚Üí- Lobby balance is the player wallet.
544‚Üí- Game CREDIT is machine-session credit.
545‚Üí- Players cash in to the machine in **200,000** increments up to **1,000,000** total cash-in per session.
546‚Üí- Players can cash out machine credits back to the wallet when the machine is closed or when machine credits reach at least **2x** the session cash-in amount.
547‚Üí- Machine closed is only a gameplay event. It does **not** reset the machine ledger or RTP memory.
548‚Üí
549‚Üí## Variance Smoothing / Fun Policy
550‚Üí
551‚ÜíThe policy layer now also reacts to long Lucky 5 droughts, net-since-close pressure, and strongly counterintuitive player behavior. This lets the online version soften very long dry spells while still using the target RTP and machine-ledger driven policy loop. The intention is **not** to force unavoidable jackpots; counterplay pressure is only a soft variance smoother so players cannot reliably exploit strange play into guaranteed jackpot outcomes. Lucky 5 itself is now intentionally stricter: the no-lose mode only arms when 5‚ôÝ is reached through SWITCH, never from the opening dealer card and never from a revealed BIG/SMALL result card.
552‚Üí
553‚Üí## Admin Tooling
554‚Üí
555‚ÜíAdmin accounts can: 
556‚Üí
557‚Üí- credit or debit player wallets without resetting machines
558‚Üí- inspect machine RTP / net-since-close / streak / jackpot state
559‚Üí- inspect per-player machine sessions and current machine credits
560‚Üí- reset machine state separately when needed

5‚Üí## Live frontend
6‚Üí
7‚ÜíThe production frontend is the vanilla app served from:
8‚Üí
9‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Api/wwwroot/index.html`
10‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Api/wwwroot/js/game.js`
11‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Api/wwwroot/css/game.css`
12‚Üí
13‚ÜíDo **not** start from the prototype folders for feature work unless you are intentionally rebuilding the frontend.
14‚Üí
15‚Üí## Core backend files
16‚Üí
17‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Api/Controllers/GameController.cs` ‚Äî gameplay, machine session cash-in/out, jackpot rank, machine state
18‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Api/Controllers/AuthController.cs` ‚Äî signup/login/OTP/profile/history
19‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Api/Controllers/AdminController.cs` ‚Äî admin-only user/machine tools
20‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Infrastructure/Services/GameService.cs` ‚Äî authoritative game flow
21‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Infrastructure/Services/AdminService.cs` ‚Äî admin balance adjustments and machine inspection/reset
22‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Infrastructure/Services/AuthService.cs` ‚Äî auth and wallet profile operations
23‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Infrastructure/Services/InMemoryDataStore.cs` ‚Äî all in-memory state
24‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Domain/Game/CleanRoom/MachinePolicy.cs` ‚Äî RTP / variance shaping / double-up deck pressure
25‚Üí
26‚Üí## Economy model
27‚Üí
28‚ÜíThere are now two balances:
29‚Üí
30‚Üí1. **Lobby wallet balance**
31‚Üí   - shown in the lobby / wallet screens
32‚Üí   - stored on `MemberProfile.WalletBalance`
33‚Üí2. **Machine credit balance**
34‚Üí   - shown on the game CREDIT display
35‚Üí   - stored per player + machine in `MachineSessionState.MachineCredits`
36‚Üí
37‚Üí### Machine session rules
38‚Üí
39‚Üí- players cash in to the machine in **200,000** increments
40‚Üí- total cash-in per machine session is capped at **1,000,000**
41‚Üí- cash-out to wallet is allowed when either:
42‚Üí  - the machine session is marked closed, or
43‚Üí  - machine credits reach at least **2x** total cash-in
44‚Üí
45‚Üí## Jackpot / double-up rules
46‚Üí
47‚Üí- jackpot values can start at max
48‚Üí- jackpot wins reset to 20% baseline values
49‚Üí- jackpot values never grow above their max caps
50‚Üí- jackpot win value goes straight to **machine credits**
51‚Üí- only the **base paytable win** enters double-up
52‚Üí- the Lucky 5 no-lose state only arms when 5‚ôÝ is reached through **SWITCH**; opening dealer 5‚ôÝ and BIG/SMALL reveal 5‚ôÝ are visual only
53‚Üí- the paytable row for the active hand is reused as the live score row during win/double-up
54‚Üí
55‚Üí## Machine closed
56‚Üí
57‚ÜíMachine closed is a gameplay event, not a hard reset.
58‚Üí
59‚Üí- machine ledgers continue accumulating history
60‚Üí- machine session is flagged closed for the current player
61‚Üí- player is expected to cash out machine credits back to the wallet
62‚Üí- machine RTP memory should keep running across closes
63‚Üí
64‚Üí## RTP / fun policy
65‚Üí
66‚Üí`MachinePolicy.cs` now uses:
67‚Üí
68‚Üí- target RTP
69‚Üí- jackpot RTP pressure (not just base paytable RTP)
70‚Üí- net since last close
71‚Üí- Lucky 5 drought
72‚Üí- loss streak pressure
73‚Üí- counterplay / counterintuitive-hold pressure
74‚Üí
75‚ÜíThis is meant to soften very long online dry spells while still letting the machine breathe with ups/downs and close misses.
76‚Üí
77‚ÜíThe latest tuning pass also tightened the negative correction when the observed RTP is already above target, so jackpot-heavy periods push the normal paytable scale down faster instead of compounding the overshoot.
78‚Üí
79‚Üí## Strange player behavior handling
80‚Üí
81‚Üí`GameService.DrawAsync()` compares actual holds against advised holds and updates `MachineSessionState.CounterplayScore`.
82‚Üí
83‚ÜíThis is then fed back into:
84‚Üí
85‚Üí- deal-time policy mode selection
86‚Üí- draw-time payout scale pressure
87‚Üí
88‚ÜíSo if a player repeatedly destroys strong hands or plays in an obviously counterintuitive way, the machine can compensate and keep the experience from becoming dead or overly punishing.
89‚Üí
90‚Üí## Admin tools
91‚Üí
92‚ÜíCurrent admin features:
93‚Üí
94‚Üí- search/list users
95‚Üí- credit or debit wallet balances with reasons
96‚Üí- inspect machine RTP and jackpot state
97‚Üí- inspect per-player machine sessions on each machine
98‚Üí- reset machine state separately from wallet adjustments
99‚Üí
100‚Üí## Adding a new lobby game
101‚Üí
102‚Üí1. Add a new entry to `AVAILABLE_GAMES` in `wwwroot/js/game.js`
103‚Üí2. Add any new route/screen handling in `showLobby()` / `openGame()`
104‚Üí3. Add the new backend controller/service if the game needs its own logic
105‚Üí4. Keep the existing Lucky5 frontend isolated so variants do not break the live cabinet
106‚Üí
107‚Üí## Known limitation / next important step
108‚Üí
109‚ÜíMachine ledgers and machine sessions still live in memory. That means they can survive for long-running single-instance deployments, but **not** across process restarts or redeploys.
110‚Üí
111‚ÜíAlso note that `DealAsync()` now requires enough machine credits for **both** deal and draw up front. That is deliberate: it avoids dead-end rounds where a player can start a hand but cannot afford the mandatory draw cost.
112‚Üí
113‚ÜíFor true month-scale durability, the next big engineering step is persistent storage for:
114‚Üí
115‚Üí- `MachineLedgerState`
116‚Üí- `MachineSessionState`
117‚Üí- optionally audit/history rows
118‚Üí
EOF

1) Current screen / menu structure
Vanilla cabinet flow (
index.html)
Primary screens are stacked in one page and toggled with .active / display:

Auth screen: login/signup bootstrap
index.html:23-83
Lobby screen: machine picker + wallet summary + bottom nav
index.html:85-131
Wallet screen: read-only balance/history view
index.html:133-154
Admin screen: player search + machine/RTP management
index.html:156-176
Game screen: core cabinet gameplay
index.html:178-293
Overlay game menu: cash in / cash out / back to lobby / reset / logout
index.html:296-303
Navigation wiring (
game.js)
Main screen transitions are direct DOM toggles:

showLobby() ‚Üí game.js:1781-1794
showWallet() ‚Üí game.js:1796-1806
showAdmin() ‚Üí game.js:1883-1895
openGame() ‚Üí game.js:1763-1779
backToLobbyFromGame() ‚Üí game.js:2004-2023
event binding bootstraps on DOM ready ‚Üí game.js:2086-2310
Styling / layout
Lobby nav + wallet/admin full-screen patterns are defined in CSS, not componentized:
lobby nav: game.css:1645-1679
wallet screen: game.css:1681-1837
admin screen: game.css:1958-2018
React src/web status
The React cabinet is useful as a play surface / telemetry prototype, but it does not currently mirror the vanilla app‚Äôs lobby/wallet/admin/menu UX:

single cabinet shell with machine buttons + diagnostics + wallet trail
no equivalent lobby nav, overlay menu, or admin flow
see src/web/components/lucky5-cabinet.tsx:131-669
So for navigation/menu/admin/player UX, the vanilla wwwroot app is still the real source of truth.

2) Player / admin menu gaps
Player gaps
‚ÄúBack to Lobby‚Äù is hidden inside the in-game menu

menu button + menu overlay: index.html:287-303
handler: game.js:2248-2250
This is discoverable only after opening the menu; there is no persistent visible back affordance in the game itself.
Wallet is lobby-only

lobby wallet CTA: 
index.html:111
wallet nav item only exists on lobby bottom bar: index.html:122-125
Once in game, wallet access is indirect via menu cash-out, not via a consistent wallet/status surface.
Menu availability is state-gated

menu opens only in idle, win, or doubleup: game.js:2190-2193
During hold, the player cannot open the menu.
That may be intentional, but it increases stuck-feeling risk during long sessions.
Machine-closed UX depends on menu wording

repeated messages instruct user to use menu for cash out: game.js:221-225, 964, 1250, 1443
If player doesn‚Äôt realize menu is the only route, recovery feels opaque.
Admin gaps
Admin is only exposed from lobby bottom nav

hidden/shown by role: index.html:126-129, game.js:1707-1710
No persistent admin shortcut once inside wallet/game.
Admin actions are prompt/confirm driven

wallet credit/debit uses prompt(): game.js:1932-1951
machine reset uses confirm(): game.js:1990-1998, 2229-2238
Functional, but brittle and low-trust for operational use.
Two reset entry points

in-game menu reset: 
index.html:301, game.js:2229-2238
admin machine-list reset: game.js:1985-1998
This duplicates mental models and risks inconsistent future behavior.
3) Button / navigation reliability risks
Highest-risk patterns
A. Manual screen toggling is duplicated and easy to drift
Different functions individually remove/add classes across screens:

openGame() ‚Üí game.js:1763-1779
showLobby() ‚Üí 1781-1794
showWallet() ‚Üí 1796-1806
showAdmin() ‚Üí 1883-1895
logout reset path ‚Üí 1642-1656
warningRisk:warning one future screen/state change can break another path because there‚Äôs no single authoritative router/state machine.

B. Menu UI is inline-styled in HTML
index.html:296-303
warningRisk:warning hard to maintain, hard to relocate ‚ÄúBack to Lobby‚Äù into a better structure, easy to visually drift from 
game.css.

C. Game re-entry is sticky via sessionStorage
machine persisted: 
game.js:1768, 2053, 2295-2300
warningRisk:warning on refresh, user is auto-dropped back into the game if lucky5_machineId exists, which can mask broken exit flows and create ‚Äúwhy am I trapped in machine view?‚Äù complaints.

D. showLobby() is async but often called like sync
async definition: 
game.js:1781
called without await from backToLobbyFromGame(): 2022
also called from login bootstrap / restore paths
This probably works, but it‚Äôs a sequencing smell because lobby rendering depends on loadAvailableMachines().

E. Nav affordances are inconsistent across contexts
lobby has bottom nav: index.html:117-130
wallet/admin only have back buttons: 
index.html:135, 158
game has only menu overlay path: index.html:296-303
warningRisk:warning too many different navigation metaphors in one app.

F. Admin visibility is client-side UX only
hide/show admin/reset by role: game.js:1707-1710
That‚Äôs fine for UX, but from a reliability perspective it means the UI layer is doing conditional exposure in multiple places instead of one centralized capability map.

4) vNext priorities
P0 ‚Äî unify navigation model
Create a single screen router/helper for:

auth
lobby
wallet
admin
game
menu open/close
Refactor targets:

game.js:1763-1806
game.js:1883-1895
game.js:2004-2023
game.js:2086-2310
Why: this is the root fix for button reliability and future menu relocation.

P1 ‚Äî move ‚ÄúBack to Lobby‚Äù out of the overlay-only pattern
Current:

button lives in menu: 
index.html:300
wired at game.js:2248-2250
Recommendation:

put a persistent cabinet-safe back/exit affordance in the game chrome
keep menu for cash in/out + logout
if user request is ‚Äúmove go-back-to-lobby into existing menu,‚Äù then at minimum restyle/reframe it as the primary exit item and make its availability clearer when machine is closed
Why: this is the most important player-navigation pain point.

P1 ‚Äî centralize machine-close recovery UX
Current machine-closed messaging is scattered:

game.js:221-225
964
1244-1250
1437-1443
Recommendation:

one helper for closed-machine state
one consistent CTA path
ensure menu/back/cash-out affordances remain reachable in every relevant post-close state
Why: directly tied to the reported invalid/close/reset/cashout confusion.

P2 ‚Äî replace admin prompt()/confirm() flows with inline panel controls
Targets:

game.js:1932-1951
game.js:1990-1998
game.js:2229-2238
Why: improves trust, prevents malformed input, and makes admin actions auditable/clear.

P2 ‚Äî bring menu styling into CSS and de-duplicate UI logic
Move menu panel/button styles from:

index.html:296-303
Into:

game.css near wallet/admin/lobby navigation sections (1645-2018 is the right neighborhood)
Why: needed before reshaping the menu without making HTML even more brittle.

P3 ‚Äî decide whether React cabinet is replacing or only shadowing vanilla
React current scope:

machine selection / deal-draw / double-up / diagnostics
src/web/components/lucky5-cabinet.tsx:256-379, 408-669
Missing vs vanilla:

lobby/wallet/admin/menu routing
session resume/back-to-lobby/menu semantics
Why: right now there are effectively two frontends, but only one complete UX. That increases future drift.

Bottom line
The vanilla wwwroot frontend already has the right broad structure, but navigation is held together by duplicated DOM toggles, an inline overlay menu, and inconsistent exit patterns. The biggest UX reliability issue is not a single broken button‚Äîit‚Äôs that screen transitions, machine recovery, and exit behavior are spread across multiple ad hoc handlers instead of one navigation model.

If you want, I can turn this into a tighter handoff artifact with:

exact bug hypotheses
recommended code touch list
verification checklist for all lobby/game/admin buttons.

1‚Üí# Final QA and RTP Pass ‚Äî 2026-03-13
2‚Üí
3‚ÜíThis document records the last debugging / optimization pass applied to the current repo snapshot.
4‚Üí
5‚Üí## What was fixed in this pass
6‚Üí
7‚Üí### 1. Deal / draw affordability bug
8‚Üí
9‚ÜíThe live frontend and backend could previously let a player start a round with only enough machine credits for the **deal** even though the game always requires a second bet for the **draw**.
10‚Üí
11‚ÜíFix:
12‚Üí
13‚Üí- `GameService.DealAsync()` now requires enough credits for **deal + draw** up front
14‚Üí- `wwwroot/js/game.js` now blocks starting the hand unless the player has at least `currentBet * 2`
15‚Üí
16‚ÜíResult:
17‚Üí
18‚Üí- no more dead-end hands where the player can deal but cannot legally draw
19‚Üí
20‚Üí### 2. Lucky 5 machine-close timing bug
21‚Üí
22‚ÜíThe older double-up logic could close the machine too early when a `5S` switch inflated the preview amount, even before a real winning guess had happened.
23‚Üí
24‚ÜíFix:
25‚Üí
26‚Üí- `Lucky5DoubleUpEngine` no longer auto-closes on session start or on switch preview
27‚Üí- machine close is now evaluated only on the **actual winning collection step**
28‚Üí- added regression coverage for the "5S switch near cap" path
29‚Üí
30‚ÜíResult:
31‚Üí
32‚Üí- `5S` can still create the dramatic preview multiplier
33‚Üí- the machine only closes when the real post-win amount would actually push collected credits over the cap
34‚Üí
35‚Üí### 3. Async blocking cleanup
36‚Üí
37‚Üí`GameService` still had `.Result` calls inside async gameplay paths.
38‚Üí
39‚ÜíFix:
40‚Üí
41‚Üí- `DoubleUpAsync()` and `GuessDoubleUpAsync()` were cleaned up to use proper `await`
42‚Üí
43‚ÜíResult:
44‚Üí
45‚Üí- lower deadlock / blocking risk in the request path
46‚Üí
47‚Üí### 4. Shared ledger locking
48‚Üí
49‚ÜíMachine/admin/game code paths were not using the same lock object.
50‚Üí
51‚ÜíFix:
52‚Üí
53‚Üí- added `InMemoryDataStore.LedgerSync`
54‚Üí- switched gameplay/admin machine-ledger mutations to the shared lock
55‚Üí
56‚ÜíResult:
57‚Üí
58‚Üí- lower risk of inconsistent machine-state mutation under concurrent access
59‚Üí
60‚Üí### 5. Jackpot-aware payout scaling
61‚Üí
62‚ÜíThe biggest RTP bug found during the pass was that the payout-scale logic was mostly looking at **base-game RTP** and only weakly correcting for jackpot overshoot.
63‚Üí
64‚ÜíFix:
65‚Üí
66‚Üí- `MachinePolicyState` now exposes `JackpotRtp`
67‚Üí- `MachinePolicy.ResolvePayoutScale()` now reduces the **base-game target** by observed jackpot RTP before deciding the scale
68‚Üí- overshoot correction was made materially stronger when live RTP is already above target
69‚Üí- fun-pressure bonuses are now damped when the machine is already overshooting
70‚Üí- win-tier spread was tightened from a wider small/medium/big separation to a narrower one
71‚Üí
72‚ÜíResult:
73‚Üí
74‚Üí- jackpot-heavy periods pull the ordinary paytable scale down faster
75‚Üí- the machine still keeps rhythm / suspense, but it does not compound overshoot as aggressively
76‚Üí
77‚Üí### 6. Double-up pressure tuning
78‚Üí
79‚ÜíThe double-up deck pressure previously leaned too much on extra aces in hot states, which is especially generous because aces are auto-win cards in the current rule set.
80‚Üí
81‚ÜíFix:
82‚Üí
83‚Üí- reduced extra ace injection in `MachinePolicy.BuildDoubleUpDeck()`
84‚Üí- left Lucky 5 pressure focused more on `5S` than on free ace-heavy decks
85‚Üí
86‚ÜíResult:
87‚Üí
88‚Üí- Lucky 5 remains visible and dramatic
89‚Üí- neutral / hot double-up becomes less automatically player-favorable than before
90‚Üí
91‚Üí### 7. Frontend machine-close guard after jackpot fill
92‚Üí
93‚ÜíA jackpot animation could finish and immediately auto-enter double-up even when the machine was already effectively closed.
94‚Üí
95‚ÜíFix:
96‚Üí
97‚Üí- `game.js` now stops the auto-start and shows `MACHINE CLOSED - TAKE SCORE / CASH OUT` if jackpot fill reaches the machine-credit limit first
98‚Üí
99‚Üí## Replay / regression updates
100‚Üí
101‚Üí- replay test setup now cashes into the machine session before dealing
102‚Üí- added coverage for the Lucky 5 switch + machine close timing path
103‚Üí
104‚Üí## Approximate RTP simulation notes used during tuning
105‚Üí
106‚ÜíA lightweight mirror simulation was used during this pass to check **behavioral direction**. It is useful for tuning, but it is still an approximation of the live service path rather than a formal authoritative production certification run.
107‚Üí
108‚ÜíThe main findings from that tuning pass were:
109‚Üí
110‚Üí- raw base-game RTP versus the full **deal + draw** cost was roughly **0.455**
111‚Üí- base game plus old payout scaling without jackpots sat around **0.883**
112‚Üí- once jackpot overlay was included, the older scale logic could overshoot badly because jackpot RTP was not being compensated enough
113‚Üí- after switching to jackpot-aware payout scaling, the mirrored total moved much closer to the target band (roughly around **0.90** in the tuning pass instead of the previous catastrophic overshoot)
114‚Üí- the biggest remaining generosity comes from **double-up**, especially with optimal or near-optimal player behavior
115‚Üí
116‚Üí## Important remaining limitation
117‚Üí
118‚ÜíThis pass improved the policy loop and removed several correctness bugs, but it does **not** claim that the current in-memory build has been mathematically certified to 87.5% across every long-run player strategy.
119‚Üí
120‚ÜíThe remaining high-variance area is still the double-up layer:
121‚Üí
122‚Üí- Ace-as-auto-win is generous
123‚Üí- optimal BIG/SMALL play remains strong
124‚Üí- true month-scale balancing should eventually be measured from a refreshed simulation harness that matches the live service rules exactly
125‚Üí
126‚Üí## Recommended next step if more tuning is needed
127‚Üí
128‚ÜíIf you want one more balancing pass after this, focus on only one thing next:
129‚Üí
130‚Üí1. refresh the simulation harness so it mirrors the live service exactly
131‚Üí2. then tune double-up EV using data rather than intuition
132‚Üí
133‚ÜíThat is the cleanest next lever if the production telemetry still trends hot.
134‚Üí
EOF

1‚Üí# Cloud Run Deployment
2‚Üí
3‚ÜíThis is the recommended easiest deployment path for Lucky5.
4‚Üí
5‚Üí## Why this is the easiest option
6‚Üí
7‚Üí- frontend and backend stay in one ASP.NET Core app
8‚Üí- no separate static hosting step is required
9‚Üí- WebSockets / SignalR work on Cloud Run
10‚Üí- the deploy script pins the app to **min 1 / max 1** to preserve in-memory machine history and RTP memory as much as possible
11‚Üí- the script auto-enables the required Google APIs and auto-detects the final Cloud Run URL
12‚Üí
13‚Üí## Before the first deploy
14‚Üí
15‚Üí1. Install the Google Cloud SDK.
16‚Üí2. Run `gcloud auth login`.
17‚Üí3. Create or choose a Google Cloud project with billing enabled.
18‚Üí4. Pick a region (the script defaults to `me-west1`).
19‚Üí
20‚Üí## One-command deploy
21‚Üí
22‚ÜíThe script can now prompt for missing values interactively, so the shortest path is simply:
23‚Üí
24‚Üí```bash
25‚Üíbash scripts/deploy-cloud-run.sh
26‚Üí```
27‚Üí
28‚ÜíIf you prefer explicit environment variables, you can still run:
29‚Üí
30‚Üí```bash
31‚ÜíPROJECT_ID=your-project \
32‚ÜíREGION=me-west1 \
33‚ÜíSERVICE_NAME=lucky5 \
34‚ÜíLUCKY5_ADMIN_USERNAME=admin \
35‚ÜíLUCKY5_ADMIN_PASSWORD=change-me-now \
36‚Üíbash scripts/deploy-cloud-run.sh
37‚Üí```
38‚Üí
39‚ÜíIf you want to allow an extra custom domain or a separate frontend origin later, add it as a comma-separated list:
40‚Üí
41‚Üí```bash
42‚ÜíPROJECT_ID=your-project \
43‚ÜíALLOWED_ORIGINS=https://your-custom-domain.com,https://your-run-url.run.app \
44‚Üíbash scripts/deploy-cloud-run.sh
45‚Üí```
46‚Üí
47‚Üí## What the script does
48‚Üí
49‚Üí- enables `run.googleapis.com`, `cloudbuild.googleapis.com`, and `artifactregistry.googleapis.com`
50‚Üí- deploys the repo directly to Cloud Run from the root `Dockerfile`
51‚Üí- sets the app to `min 1 / max 1` with session affinity
52‚Üí- reads the final service URL and uses it as the default allowed origin when you do not provide one
53‚Üí- prompts for project / region / service / admin credentials when you do not pre-set them in the shell
54‚Üí
55‚Üí## Important note about machine history
56‚Üí
57‚ÜíThe machine ledger does **not** reset when a machine closes. Machine closed is only a gameplay event. However, the repo still stores machine history in memory, so a full process restart or redeploy can still clear history. For truly month-scale durability, the next step is persistent storage for machine ledgers and machine sessions.
58‚Üí
59‚Üí## Optional next improvements
60‚Üí
61‚Üí- move machine ledgers and sessions into Cloud SQL or Firestore for durable history
62‚Üí- add a custom domain on Cloud Run
63‚Üí- store admin credentials in Secret Manager instead of plain environment variables
64‚Üí
EOF

1‚Üí# Azure Deployment Guide for Lucky5-v7
2‚Üí
3‚Üí## Overview
4‚Üí
5‚ÜíLucky5 should be deployed to Azure as a **single-region Azure App Service running the repo's root Dockerfile**, with **GitHub Actions building and deploying on pushes to `main`**.
6‚Üí
7‚ÜíThis is the supported production path because:
8‚Üí
9‚Üí- the backend is an ASP.NET Core 9 app
10‚Üí- the frontend is served from the same backend host
11‚Üí- SignalR/WebSockets need an always-running web app
12‚Üí- machine sessions, RTP memory, jackpots, and admin state are still stored in memory, so scale-out and multi-region are unsafe today
13‚Üí- GitHub-based CI/CD avoids uploading a local working tree for every deployment
14‚Üí
15‚Üí## Supported deployment model
16‚Üí
17‚Üí### One-time Azure bootstrap
18‚Üí
19‚ÜíCreate or reuse these Azure resources in one region:
20‚Üí
21‚Üí- one resource group
22‚Üí- one Azure Container Registry
23‚Üí- one Linux App Service plan
24‚Üí- one Linux Web App configured for a custom container
25‚Üí
26‚ÜíThe Web App should use a system-assigned managed identity with `AcrPull` on the registry.
27‚Üí
28‚Üí### Ongoing deployments
29‚Üí
30‚ÜíThe repo's GitHub Actions workflow at `.github/workflows/deploy-azure-app-service.yml` is the active deployment path.
31‚Üí
32‚ÜíOn every push to `main`, it:
33‚Üí
34‚Üí1. logs into Azure using GitHub OIDC
35‚Üí2. builds the root `Dockerfile`
36‚Üí3. pushes the image to Azure Container Registry
37‚Üí4. ensures the App Service identity can pull from ACR
38‚Üí5. updates the Web App to the new image
39‚Üí6. checks `https://<app-name>.azurewebsites.net/health/live`
40‚Üí
41‚Üí## Workflow configuration
42‚Üí
43‚ÜíThe workflow currently targets one concrete production deployment:
44‚Üí
45‚Üí- subscription: `343ce01c-0b2a-46a3-bb0e-24c0469a9bfe`
46‚Üí- resource group: `lucky5-rg`
47‚Üí- region: `westeurope`
48‚Üí- Azure Container Registry: `lucky520260401001454`
49‚Üí- Azure Web App: `lucky5-ik90-prod-20260401`
50‚Üí
51‚ÜíThese values are committed directly in the workflow because they are infrastructure identifiers, not secrets. If you rename or recreate the Azure resources later, update the workflow file to match.
52‚Üí
53‚Üí## Required Azure configuration
54‚Üí
55‚ÜíBefore GitHub Actions can deploy, Azure must have:
56‚Üí
57‚Üí- a Microsoft Entra application / service principal for GitHub Actions
58‚Üí- a federated credential for `repo:ironkid90/Lucky5-v7:ref:refs/heads/main`
59‚Üí- Azure RBAC allowing that principal to:
60‚Üí  - push images to ACR
61‚Üí  - update the Lucky5 Web App
62‚Üí
63‚Üí## App settings the deployment preserves
64‚Üí
65‚ÜíThe workflow ensures these runtime settings remain present:
66‚Üí
67‚Üí- `WEBSITES_PORT=8080`
68‚Üí- `PORT=8080`
69‚Üí- `WEBSITES_ENABLE_APP_SERVICE_STORAGE=false`
70‚Üí- `ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production`
71‚Üí- `CORS__ALLOWED_ORIGINS=https://<app-name>.azurewebsites.net`
72‚Üí
73‚ÜíAdmin credentials should be set directly on the Web App in Azure and should not be committed to GitHub.
74‚Üí
75‚Üí## Manual trigger
76‚Üí
77‚ÜíYou can also run the deployment manually from GitHub Actions with `workflow_dispatch`.
78‚Üí
79‚Üí## Post-deployment checks
80‚Üí
81‚ÜíAfter deployment, verify:
82‚Üí
83‚Üí- app root: `https://<your-app-name>.azurewebsites.net`
84‚Üí- health: `https://<your-app-name>.azurewebsites.net/health/live`
85‚Üí- SignalR hub: `https://<your-app-name>.azurewebsites.net/CarrePokerGameHub`
86‚Üí
87‚ÜíTo follow logs live:
88‚Üí
89‚Üí```bash
90‚Üíaz webapp log tail --resource-group <resource-group> --name <your-app-name>
91‚Üí```
92‚Üí
93‚Üí## Important production notes
94‚Üí
95‚Üí- Keep the deployment at **one instance**. Do not scale out horizontally yet.
96‚Üí- A redeploy or app restart can still clear machine state because persistence is still in memory.
97‚Üí- Multi-region deployment is not supported yet. See `AZURE_MULTI_REGION_GUIDE.md`.
98‚Üí- If you add a custom domain later, update `CORS__ALLOWED_ORIGINS` to include it.
99‚Üí
100‚Üí## Legacy note
101‚Üí
102‚ÜíThe older local helper script `scripts/setup-azure.sh` reflects the previous local-machine-driven deployment flow. It is no longer the recommended production deployment path for Lucky5.
103‚Üí
104‚ÜíThis guide was updated on 2026-04-01.
105‚Üí

1‚Üí# AZURE_MULTI_REGION_GUIDE
2‚Üí
3‚Üí## Current status
4‚Üí
5‚ÜíLucky5 should **not** be deployed in active/active multi-region mode yet.
6‚Üí
7‚ÜíThe current production architecture still keeps these concerns in memory inside the app process:
8‚Üí
9‚Üí- machine sessions
10‚Üí- machine ledgers and RTP memory
11‚Üí- jackpots
12‚Üí- admin state
13‚Üí- user/session runtime state used by gameplay flows
14‚Üí
15‚ÜíIf you run multiple regions at the same time today, those regions will drift apart and players can land on different copies of the game state.
16‚Üí
17‚Üí## Supported Azure deployment today
18‚Üí
19‚ÜíUse the single-region deployment documented in `AZURE_DEPLOYMENT_GUIDE.md` and automated by:
20‚Üí
21‚Üí```bash
22‚Üíbash scripts/setup-azure.sh
23‚Üí```
24‚Üí
25‚ÜíKeep the app on one instance in one region until the state model is externalized.
26‚Üí
27‚Üí## What must exist before multi-region is safe
28‚Üí
29‚ÜíBefore enabling multi-region, Lucky5 needs at least:
30‚Üí
31‚Üí1. durable shared persistence for machine ledgers, jackpots, machine sessions, and auth/session state
32‚Üí2. a clear cross-region consistency strategy for gameplay writes
33‚Üí3. SignalR/session fan-out strategy across instances or a managed realtime backplane
34‚Üí4. traffic routing and failover that preserve session affinity expectations
35‚Üí5. explicit recovery rules for in-flight rounds during failover or redeploy
36‚Üí
37‚Üí## Suggested future Azure shape
38‚Üí
39‚ÜíOnce state is externalized, a safer multi-region Azure architecture would look like:
40‚Üí
41‚Üí- one deployment per region
42‚Üí- shared durable data store for authoritative game state
43‚Üí- shared cache/backplane only where required
44‚Üí- Azure Front Door or Traffic Manager for geo-routing and failover
45‚Üí- health probes and operational playbooks for regional failover
46‚Üí
47‚Üí## Practical recommendation
48‚Üí
49‚ÜíFor now:
50‚Üí
51‚Üí- deploy one region only
52‚Üí- keep one app instance only
53‚Üí- treat restarts and redeploys as state-reset events until persistence is added
54‚Üí
55‚ÜíWhen the backend moves off the in-memory store, this guide can be expanded into a real multi-region rollout document.
EOF

1‚Üí# Android APK Build Guide
2‚Üí
3‚Üí## Overview
4‚Üí
5‚ÜíThe Lucky5 Android app is a **Capacitor wrapper** around the existing web frontend.
6‚ÜíIt packages the HTML/CSS/JS cabinet UI into a native Android WebView so the game
7‚Üícan be installed and launched as a regular Android app.
8‚Üí
9‚ÜíThe game logic remains **backend-authoritative** ‚Äî the Android app connects to
10‚Üíthe Lucky5 API server over the network, exactly like the browser version.
11‚Üí
12‚Üí## Prerequisites
13‚Üí
14‚Üí| Tool            | Version   | Notes                                          |
15‚Üí|-----------------|-----------|-------------------------------------------------|
16‚Üí| Node.js         | ‚â• 18      | for Capacitor CLI and sync scripts              |
17‚Üí| npm             | ‚â• 9       | ships with Node                                 |
18‚Üí| Android Studio  | ‚â• 2024.x  | for building the APK / running on device        |
19‚Üí| JDK             | 17+       | required by AGP (Android Gradle Plugin)         |
20‚Üí
21‚Üí> **Tip**: Install Android Studio from https://developer.android.com/studio.
22‚Üí> Accept SDK licenses via `sdkmanager --licenses` if building from the CLI.
23‚Üí
24‚Üí## Quick Start
25‚Üí
26‚Üí```bash
27‚Üí# 1 ‚Äî Install npm dependencies
28‚Üícd mobile
29‚Üínpm install
30‚Üí
31‚Üí# 2 ‚Äî Configure API server URL
32‚Üí#     Copy the template and edit with your server address
33‚Üícp env.example.json env.json
34‚Üí#     Edit env.json ‚Üí set "apiUrl" to your server (e.g. "https://lucky5.example.com")
35‚Üí
36‚Üí# 3 ‚Äî Sync web assets & Capacitor Android project
37‚Üínpm run build
38‚Üí#     This copies wwwroot ‚Üí mobile/www (patching paths), then syncs to Android.
39‚Üí
40‚Üí# 4 ‚Äî Open in Android Studio
41‚Üínpm run cap:open
42‚Üí#     Build & run from Android Studio, or use the CLI:
43‚Üí#     npx cap run android
44‚Üí```
45‚Üí
46‚Üí## Build Commands
47‚Üí
48‚Üí| Command              | Description                                          |
49‚Üí|----------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
50‚Üí| `npm run sync-web`   | Copy & patch web assets from wwwroot ‚Üí mobile/www    |
51‚Üí| `npm run build`      | sync-web + Capacitor sync to Android                 |
52‚Üí| `npm run cap:sync`   | Same as build (alias)                                |
53‚Üí| `npm run cap:open`   | Open Android project in Android Studio               |
54‚Üí| `npm run cap:run`    | Build & deploy to connected device/emulator          |
55‚Üí
56‚Üí## API URL Configuration
57‚Üí
58‚ÜíThe web app needs to know where the Lucky5 backend is running. There are two ways
59‚Üíto configure this:
60‚Üí
61‚Üí### Option A: `env.json` file (recommended)
62‚Üí
63‚ÜíCreate `mobile/env.json`:
64‚Üí```json
65‚Üí{
66‚Üí  "apiUrl": "https://lucky5.example.com"
67‚Üí}
68‚Üí```
69‚Üí
70‚Üí### Option B: CLI flag
71‚Üí
72‚Üí```bash
73‚Üínode scripts/sync-web.mjs --api-url "https://lucky5.example.com"
74‚Üí```
75‚Üí
76‚Üí> **Important**: `env.json` is git-ignored. Each developer sets their own server URL.
77‚Üí
78‚Üí## Project Structure
79‚Üí
80‚Üí```
81‚Üímobile/
82‚Üí‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ package.json              # npm project & build scripts
83‚Üí‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ capacitor.config.json     # Capacitor configuration
84‚Üí‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ env.example.json          # API URL template (committed)
85‚Üí‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ env.json                  # Local API URL override (git-ignored)
86‚Üí‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ scripts/
87‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ sync-web.mjs          # Copies wwwroot ‚Üí www with path patches
88‚Üí‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ www/                       # Synced web assets (git-ignored, generated)
89‚Üí‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ android/                   # Native Android project (Capacitor-managed)
90‚Üí    ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ app/
91‚Üí    ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ src/main/
92‚Üí    ‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ AndroidManifest.xml
93‚Üí    ‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ java/com/lucky5/videopoker/
94‚Üí    ‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ MainActivity.java
95‚Üí    ‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ assets/public/      # Web assets (synced, git-ignored)
96‚Üí    ‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ res/                # Android resources (icons, themes)
97‚Üí    ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ build.gradle
98‚Üí    ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ build.gradle
99‚Üí    ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ gradle/
100‚Üí    ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ variables.gradle
101‚Üí```
102‚Üí
103‚Üí## Building a Release APK
104‚Üí
105‚ÜíOnce the debug workflow is working:
106‚Üí
107‚Üí```bash
108‚Üí# 1 ‚Äî Sync assets
109‚Üícd mobile
110‚Üínpm run build
111‚Üí
112‚Üí# 2 ‚Äî Build release APK via Gradle
113‚Üícd android
114‚Üí./gradlew assembleRelease
115‚Üí
116‚Üí# Output: android/app/build/outputs/apk/release/app-release-unsigned.apk
117‚Üí```
118‚Üí
119‚Üí### Signing the APK
120‚Üí
EOF

5‚Üí## Purpose
6‚Üí
7‚ÜíThis document consolidates the current Lucky5 codebase shape, the relevant documentation findings, and a practical vNext roadmap focused on:
8‚Üí
9‚Üí1. durable persistence
10‚Üí2. better player/admin menu flows
11‚Üí3. supporting utilities and operational hardening
12‚Üí4. moving Lucky5 from a strong prototype into a more deployment-ready product

16‚Üí## Executive Summary
17‚Üí
18‚ÜíLucky5 already has the core ingredients of a serious product:
19‚Üí
20‚Üí- authoritative backend gameplay
21‚Üí- deterministic clean-room engine
22‚Üí- Lebanese cabinet-accurate vanilla frontend
23‚Üí- admin tooling
24‚Üí- machine-session economy
25‚Üí- RTP / jackpot / double-up policy systems
26‚Üí- Android wrapper path
27‚Üí- deployable single-service hosting model
28‚Üí
29‚ÜíBut the repo is still bottlenecked by one major architectural limitation:
30‚Üí
31‚Üí> **authoritative runtime state is still in memory**
32‚Üí
33‚ÜíThat limitation cascades into nearly every remaining completion gap:
34‚Üí
35‚Üí- machine history is lost on restart/redeploy
36‚Üí- single-instance hosting is mandatory
37‚Üí- multi-region is unsafe
38‚Üí- in-flight round recovery is weak
39‚Üí- admin operations lack durable audit depth
40‚Üí- long-session continuity is fragile
41‚Üí
42‚ÜíAt the same time, the live frontend is feature-rich but navigation is still managed by duplicated DOM toggles and an overlay menu pattern that makes some core actions feel hidden or brittle.
43‚Üí
44‚Üí**vNext should therefore be organized around three primary tracks:**
45‚Üí
46‚Üí- **Track A ‚Äî Persistence & recovery**
47‚Üí- **Track B ‚Äî Navigation/menu/admin UX hardening**
48‚Üí- **Track C ‚Äî Supporting utilities, observability, and delivery readiness**

278‚Üí## Recommended vNext Workstreams
279‚Üí
280‚Üí## Track A ‚Äî Persistence & Recovery Foundation (P0)
281‚Üí
282‚Üí### Goal
283‚Üí
284‚ÜíReplace volatile authoritative state with durable storage while preserving existing gameplay rules and economy semantics.
285‚Üí
286‚Üí### Persist first
287‚Üí
288‚Üí1. `MachineLedgerState`
289‚Üí2. `MachineSessionState`
290‚Üí3. `GameRound` (at least active + recent settled rounds)
291‚Üí4. `WalletLedgerEntry`
292‚Üí5. `User`
293‚Üí6. `MemberProfile`
294‚Üí
295‚Üí### Why this order
296‚Üí
297‚Üí- machine-ledger and machine-session durability are explicitly the biggest blocker
298‚Üí- round durability is needed for reconnect/recovery and anti-duplication
299‚Üí- wallet/user/profile persistence is straightforward and unlocks cleaner admin/reporting flows
300‚Üí
301‚Üí### Suggested storage boundary
302‚Üí
303‚ÜíIntroduce repository interfaces beneath services so that:
304‚Üí
305‚Üí- `GameService`
306‚Üí- `AuthService`
307‚Üí- `AdminService`
308‚Üí
309‚Üístop directly depending on `InMemoryDataStore` as the long-term source of truth.
310‚Üí
311‚Üí### Recommended persistence shape
312‚Üí
313‚Üí**Phase A1 ‚Äî Hybrid persistence**
314‚Üí
315‚Üí- keep in-memory caching where useful
316‚Üí- add durable backing store for authoritative writes
317‚Üí- hydrate cache on startup
318‚Üí
319‚Üí**Phase A2 ‚Äî Repository-first**
320‚Üí
321‚Üí- move services to repository interfaces
322‚Üí- use in-memory implementation only for tests/dev fallback
323‚Üí
324‚Üí### Must-have behaviors
325‚Üí
326‚Üí- restart-safe machine ledger history
327‚Üí- restart-safe machine credit sessions
328‚Üí- active round recovery on reconnect
329‚Üí- idempotent payout/cashout on retry
330‚Üí- machine close remains gameplay event, not ledger reset
331‚Üí- reset operations become explicit durable actions, not just memory wipes
332‚Üí
333‚Üí### File areas impacted
334‚Üí
335‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Infrastructure/Services/InMemoryDataStore.cs`
336‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Infrastructure/Services/GameService.cs`
337‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Infrastructure/Services/AdminService.cs`
338‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Infrastructure/Services/AuthService.cs`
339‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Domain/Entities/*`
340‚Üí- new repository / persistence implementations under `server/src/Lucky5.Infrastructure/`
341‚Üí
342‚Üí### Acceptance criteria
343‚Üí
344‚Üí- restart does not erase machine ledger
345‚Üí- restart does not erase machine session credits
346‚Üí- active round can be resumed or safely closed after restart
347‚Üí- admin machine inspection reflects persisted truth
348‚Üí- cash-in/cash-out/double-up settlements remain idempotent
349‚Üí

351‚Üí
352‚Üí## Track B ‚Äî Navigation, Menu, and Cabinet UX Hardening (P1)
353‚Üí
354‚Üí### Goal
355‚Üí
356‚ÜíMake every player/admin route explicit, testable, and consistent.
357‚Üí
358‚Üí### Core tasks
359‚Üí
360‚Üí#### B1. Introduce a single frontend router/state transition layer
361‚Üí
362‚ÜíRefactor all screen switching into one authoritative helper.
363‚Üí
364‚ÜíCurrent scattered areas:
365‚Üí
366‚Üí- `openGame()`
367‚Üí- `showLobby()`
368‚Üí- `showWallet()`
369‚Üí- `showAdmin()`
370‚Üí- logout/reset transitions
371‚Üí- menu open/close transitions
372‚Üí
373‚Üí### Why
374‚Üí
375‚ÜíThis is the root cause behind button drift and broken-path risk.
376‚Üí
377‚Üí#### B2. Normalize in-game menu structure
378‚Üí
379‚ÜíThe menu should remain part of the cabinet experience, but its actions need clearer prioritization.
380‚Üí
381‚ÜíRecommended shape:
382‚Üí
383‚Üí- CASH IN
384‚Üí- CASH OUT
385‚Üí- BACK TO LOBBY / EXIT MACHINE

388‚Üí- CLOSE

390‚Üí### Why
391‚Üí
392‚ÜíCurrently ‚ÄúBack to Lobby‚Äù is functionally available but too hidden/fragile.
393‚Üí
394‚Üí#### B3. Centralize machine-closed recovery UX
395‚Üí

397‚Üí

403‚Üí### Why
404‚Üí

406‚Üí
407‚Üí#### B4. Remove inline-style menu brittleness
408‚Üí
409‚ÜíMove remaining inline menu structure/styling into CSS and keep HTML semantic.
410‚Üí
411‚Üí#### B5. Decide vanilla vs React ownership
412‚Üí
413‚ÜíEither:
414‚Üí
415‚Üí- keep vanilla as canonical and React as future rebuild target, or
416‚Üí- commit to React parity work as a formal project

418‚ÜíBut do not keep them in ambiguous dual ownership.
419‚Üí

423‚Üí- `server/src/Lucky5.Api/wwwroot/js/game.js`

427‚Üí### Acceptance criteria
428‚Üí
429‚Üí- every screen transition uses one shared routing helper

597‚Üí## Recommended Definition of Done for vNext
598‚Üí
599‚ÜíLucky5 vNext should not be considered complete until all of the following are true:
600‚Üí
601‚Üí- authoritative runtime state is durable across restart/redeploy
602‚Üí- machine sessions survive process restarts
603‚Üí- machine ledgers survive process restarts
604‚Üí- wallet history is durable and inspectable
605‚Üí- active rounds can be recovered or safely reconciled
606‚Üí- all cabinet/lobby/admin buttons have a single verified navigation model
607‚Üí- machine-close / cashout / reset semantics are unambiguous in UI and backend
608‚Üí- admin actions are auditable and no longer rely on prompt/confirm dialogs
609‚Üí- deployment guidance can safely describe the app as durable in single-region production
610‚Üí- the React path is either promoted with parity scope or explicitly demoted to secondary status
